title: Lavender moth sachets
subtitle: Dried lavender flowers in muslin, boosted with lavender essential oil
excerpt: Lavender moth deterrent sachets filled with dried lavender flowers and boosted with a few drops of lavender essential oil. Clothes moths (Tineola bisselliella) are repelled by the linalool and linalyl acetate in lavender. Hang in wardrobes or place in drawers near woollens and natural-fibre garments.
sourceNotes: Pest Action UK, Natural Moth Control advice (pestaction.org.uk, May 2026). Essential Oil Safety, Tisserand and Young (2014), lavender in non-skin-contact applications. WRAP, Caring for Clothes guide (wrap.org.uk, 2023).

recipe.batchNotes: Scale to number of sachets needed. One wardrobe typically needs 2-3 sachets; one hanging from the rail and one in each lower shelf area.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Ready immediately. Refresh the essential oil every 3-4 months as the scent fades.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: 12 months of active deterrence. Dried lavender retains volatile linalool for approximately 12 months; after that, squeeze the sachet; if little scent is released the lavender is exhausted and the sachet needs replacing or refreshing. Refreshing: add 3-4 drops of lavender essential oil directly onto the outside of the sachet fabric to extend effectiveness without refilling.

content.text: A lavender moth sachet. Clothes moths avoid laying eggs in areas where lavender's primary volatile compounds (linalool and linalyl acetate) are present. The sachets act as a deterrent, not a killer; they repel adult moths from laying eggs in your woollens rather than exterminating an existing infestation. For an active infestation (visible larvae, feeding damage, or webbing in garments), freeze affected items for 3 days to kill eggs and larvae, then wash before deploying sachets as ongoing prevention.

The essential oil boost doubles the effective linalool concentration in a sachet of dried lavender flowers, extending the active deterrence period. Without the boost, dried lavender alone begins to lose effectiveness after 4-6 months as the oils exhaust. The sachet fabric should be loosely woven enough to allow the fragrance to permeate; muslin is ideal. A tightly woven fabric traps the fragrance inside the sachet.

items.prepNote: dried Lavandula angustifolia flowers. Enough for 4-6 sachets at 5-8 g each. The dried buds should be small and dense; loose stems and woody bits reduce the effective flower weight. Rub a pinch between the fingers; strong scent indicates good volatile oil content.

Lavandula angustifolia lavender essential oil. Applied directly to the dried flowers before filling, or added to the outside of the finished sachets. Boosts linalool concentration and extends active deterrence to 12 months.

content.text: Measure the dried lavender flowers into a small bowl. If using the essential oil, sprinkle the drops evenly over the flowers and stir through. Leave for 5 minutes for the oil to absorb into the dry flowers before filling the sachets.

Cut the muslin cloth into 15 × 15 cm squares. Place 5-8 g of lavender fill in the centre of each square. Gather the corners and tie tightly with string, ribbon, or a rubber band. Alternatively, sew or iron-seal three sides of a muslin sachet bag before filling, then close the fourth side. Pre-made small muslin bags (7 × 9 cm, widely available online) are a convenient alternative to cutting your own.

Hang one sachet from each wardrobe rail, next to (not touching) woollen garments. Place one or two flat sachets on each shelf where natural-fibre items are stored. Replace sachets every 12 months, or refresh by squeezing the sachet firmly to crack the dried flowers and release fresh volatiles, followed by 3-4 drops of lavender essential oil onto the outside of the fabric.
12 months of active deterrence in a wardrobe. Unused sachets store indefinitely in a sealed bag; the enclosed environment keeps the volatile oils from escaping until the sachet is deployed.
